content.full_text:
REED & DAVIDSON
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
666 WEST SIXTH STREET, 12TH FLOOR
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017

DANA W. REED
CARY DAVIDSON
DARYL W. WOLD
OF COUNSEL

TELEPHONE (213) 362-9238
FACSIMILE (213) 623-1892

ORANGE COUNTY OFFICE
351 AIRWAY AVENUE, SUITE M1
CORONA, CALIFORNIA 91720
TELEPHONE (714) 641-688
FACSIMILE (714) 546-003

To: The Honorable Members of the Fair Political Practices
Commission

From: Reed and Davidson

Re: Agenda Item #8, Advice Letter A-89-533

Date: November 7, 1989

================================================================

This office represents numerous ballot measure committees,

many of which are controlled by one or more officeholders.


We sincerely appreciate the Commission's attempt to clarify

and interpret recent amendments to the Political Reform Act.

We feel, however, that Advice Letter A-89-533 dated

October 10, 1989 by Scott Hallabrin, Esq. is in need of

further consideration.


In his letter, Mr. Hallabrin states that under Section 85302

of the Act, a candidate controlled committee's contribution

to a ballot measure committee from campaign funds raised

after January 1, 1989 is permissible as long as the ballot

measure committee is not controlled by a candidate. Section

85302, however, purports to prohibit contributions in excess
of $2,500 to committees making contributions to candidates
for elective office. It is difficult to understand Mr.
Hallabrin's rationale in determining that Section 85302's
prohibition against contributing more than $2,500 to
candidate committees makes permissible contributions to
ballot measure committees "...as long as the ballot measure
committee is not controlled by a candidate."

     A.  Are contributions under $2,500 to candidate
controlled ballot measure committees by candidate controlled
candidate committees permissible?

     B.  Are candidate controlled committee
contributions to non-candidate controlled ballot measure
committees limited by this section to $2,500?

     C.  Is Mr. Hallabrin saying that a
candidate/officeholder is prohibited from contributing funds
from his controlled campaign or officeholder account to a
ballot measure account which he also controls?

          a.  If the answer above is no, what if the
officeholder only partially controls the committee along
with other officeholders?


In a confirming letter to Mr. Hallabrin dated September 7,

1989, which precipitated Mr. Hallabrin's response, Mr.

Thomas W. Hiltachk cites Section 85202 (not Section 85302)

as the controlling section. Section 85202 purports to (a)

prohibit candidates from accepting contributions from

sources not enumerated in that section and (b) require that

all funds collected be held in trust for expenses associated

with the election to the specific office sought. It is

equally unclear to us how Mr. Hallabrin could conclude (if

indeed Mr. Hiltachk is correct) that Section 85202 allows

transfers to ballot measure committees only if said ballot

measure committee "...is not controlled by a candidate."


Our position is that contributions to ballot measure

committees are protected by United States Supreme Court

decisions such as First National Bank of Boston v. Bellotti

(1978) 435 U.S. 765, 98 S.Ct. 1407, 55 L.Ed2d 707 and

Citizens Against Rent Control v. City of Berkeley (1981) 454

U.S. 290, 102 S.Ct. 434, 70 L.Ed2d 492. Mr. Hallabrin's

attempt to limit contributions to ballot measure committees

based upon either Section 85302 or 85202 does not, in our

opinion, appear to meet the tests of Bellotti or Berkeley.


Thank you for your consideration of this issue.

League of California Cities

Sacramento, California
November 16, 1989

Via Telecopy and Delivery

Gregory Baugher
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Request for Advice

Dear Mr. Baugher:

Pursuant to Section 18329 of title 2 of the California Code of Regulations, the League of California Cities is writing to request the advice of the Fair Political Practices Commission on several issues regarding local elected official participation in initiative campaigns.

The League is writing as a representative of California cities and their elected officials. It has formed a task force to evaluate options open to cities in participating in the initiative process. This task force is chaired by Greg Cox, the Mayor of Chula Vista; its members are Al Urias, Council Member for Santa Paula; Joy Picus, Council Member for Los Angeles; Karen Humphrey, Mayor of Fresno; and Jane Baker, Council Member for San Mateo. See 2 Cal. Code of Reg. s 18329(b)(1).

As the Commission is aware, important public policy decisions are increasingly being made by the electorate through the initiative process. Cities and their local elected officials are being asked to participate in initiative campaigns, both before initiatives qualify for the ballot and after. Thus, local elected officials need to understand how campaign finance laws and the Political Reform Act affect their ability to participate in this process.

The issues on which the League seeks advice are as follows:

1. May local elected officials use their campaign funds to contribute to the qualification of initiatives for the ballot?

2. May they use such funds to support or oppose an initiative which has already qualified for the ballot?

3. Does it matter when the campaign funds were collected (e.g., before or after January 1, 1989)?

CONFERENCE REGISTRATION OFFICE
BOX 7005, LAFAYETTE, CA 94549
(415) 283-2113

HEADQUARTERS
1400 K STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 444-5790

SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA OFFICE
404 HILTON CENTER OFFICE BLDG.
900 WILSHIRE BOULEVARD
LOS ANGELES, CA 90017
(213) 629-1426

4.      How should any such contributions from campaign funds be reported?

5.      What are the restrictions and reporting requirements if a local elected
        official raises money as an intermediary for an initiative campaign?

6.      Do any of these answers change if the initiative campaign being
        contributed to is sponsored by another elected official or candidate for
        public office?

7.      Does the Political Reform Act contain any other restrictions on local
        officials participation in initiative campaigns?

Thank you for your time and attention to this inquiry. The Commission's
response will be of great assistance to local elected officials throughout the state.

                                                Very truly yours,

                                                [signature]
                                                Don Benninghoven
                                                Executive Director

TITLE 2    FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION    § 18320
(Register 88, No. 41—10-6-88)    (p. 308.1)

18319. Delegation of Authority.
Except as otherwise provided, any authority imposed upon the Executive
Director by regulations adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission may
be delegated in writing to any member of the Commission staff or any person
contracting with the Commission.

NOTE: Authority cited: Section 83112, Government Code. Reference: Section 83108,
Government Code.

HISTORY:
1. New section filed 11-2-78; effective thirtieth day thereafter (Register 78, No. 44).
2. Amendment of section heading filed 10-29-81; effective thirtieth day thereafter
(Register 81, No. 44).

18320. Requests for Opinions.
(a) Opinion requests may be submitted to the Commission by any person
whose duties under the Act are in question or by that person's representative.
(1) The identity of the person whose duties are in question shall be provided
with the opinion request. If the opinion request is submitted by the representative of several persons similarly situated, the identity of at least one such person
shall be provided with the opinion request.
(2) The immunity provided by Government Code Section 83114 shall extend
only to the person or persons identified in the opinion request.
(b) Opinion requests shall present all material facts as concisely as possible
and shall state the question or questions based on the facts.
(c) The Commission staff shall maintain a master file containing all opinion
requests. This file shall be open to public inspection.
(d) The Executive Director shall determine whether to grant or deny opinion requests.
(e) Within 14 days after the request is submitted, the person making the
opinion request shall be notified in writing of the decision of the Executive
Director. If the opinion request is denied, the notification shall state the reason
for the denial and shall advise the person submitting the request of his right to
appeal to the Commission pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.
(f) Among the criteria upon which denial of an opinion request may be based
are the following:
(1) The question raised is covered by Commission regulations.
(2) The question raises no substantial question of interpretation and, therefore, requires only a routine reply more appropriately made by staff.
(3) The person who made the request does not have sufficient interest
(financial) in the question to justify an opinion being issued.
(4) The question is hypothetical. However, opinion requests may be granted
if the hypothetical facts stated represent an intended course of conduct which
is contingent on the Commission's opinion.
(5) The question is overbroad in that it asks for an interpretation of the Act
in general terms.
(6) The request does not present material facts and does not state a question
based on the facts presented.

14 DAYS FROM REQUEST

We sincerely appreciate the Commission's attempt to clarify and interpret recent amendments to the Political Reform Act. We feel, however, that Advice Letter A-89-533 dated October 10, 1989 by Scott Hallabrin, Esq. is in need of further consideration. In his letter, Mr. Hallabrin states that under Section 85302 of the Act, a candidate controlled committee's contribution to a ballot measure committee from campaign funds raised after January 1, 1989 is permissible as long as the ballot measure committee is not controlled by a candidate. Section 85302, however, purports to prohibit contributions in excess of $2,500 to committees making contributions to candidates for elective office. It is difficult to understand Mr. Hallabrin's rationale in determining that Section 85302's prohibition against contributing more than $2,500 to candidate committees makes permissible contributions to ballot measure committees "...as long as the ballot measure committee is not controlled by a candidate." A. Are contributions under $2,500 to candidate controlled ballot measure committees by candidate controlled candidate committees permissible? B. Are candidate controlled committee contributions to non-candidate controlled ballot measure committees limited by this section to $2,500? C. Is Mr. Hallabrin saying that a candidate/officeholder is prohibited from contributing funds from his controlled campaign or officeholder account to a ballot measure account which he also controls? a. If the answer above is no, what if the officeholder only partially controls the committee along with other officeholders? In a confirming letter to Mr. Hallabrin dated September 7, 1989, which precipitated Mr. Hallabrin's response, Mr. Thomas W. Hiltachk cites Section 85202 (not Section 85302) as the controlling section. Section 85202 purports to (a) prohibit candidates from accepting contributions from sources not enumerated in that section and (b) require that all funds collected be held in trust for expenses associated with the election to the specific office sought. It is equally unclear to us how Mr. Hallabrin could conclude (if indeed Mr. Hiltachk is correct) that Section 85202 allows transfers to ballot measure committees only if said ballot measure committee "...is not controlled by a candidate." Our position is that contributions to ballot measure committees are protected by United States Supreme Court decisions such as First National Bank of Boston v. Thank you for your consideration of this issue. **League of California Cities** Sacramento, California November 16, 1989 Via Telecopy and Delivery Gregory Baugher Executive Director Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 P.O.
